item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kinda like it, but my immediate problem with this is its length - paragraphs upon paragraphs of text with little formatting to separate them, with a lot of the text itself feeling like fluff. There's so much text to even get to the point of the SCP that it completely loses the impact you normally get, and then continues to drag on without really adding much else. I quite like the meat of the article itself; cutting the length in half and restructuring the pacing to keep the reader engaged would make this an easy +1 for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Here’s a fun fact for anyone reading this, the drug that “dr Smith” asked for in the interview “triptacederine” is apparently a fictional painkiller from Star Trek.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmmm. I just learned about a British TV show called Dr. Finlay, though that's spelled a bit differently, and the whole "getting excited and treating patients with weird stuff" reminds me of House. Is the mysterious colleague also a TV doctor? I feel like it's a reference to something, but I can't put my finger on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well done!
There's one more, but it's very carefully hidden so the average reader isn't likely to find it. And it does actually tie in with the story (a little bit).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love medical dramas! Let me see if I understood all those references.

  • Drs. Howard & Fine: Three Stooges
  • Dr. Hackenbush: Groucho Marx
  • Dr. Quinn: Medicine Woman
  • Dr. Pierce: Grey's Anatomy? M*A*S*H?

And of course Dr. Smith/Lost in Space, as mentioned in the footnote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Quick note OP.

Ten grams of spores per breath is a LOT of spores. That's a kilogram every ten minutes or so, or an entire body mass within a day.

Ten milligrams seems more appropriate.
